summary: Fournier shares advice that transformed her leadership approach: replacing aggressive questioning with genuine curiosity. She acknowledges her natural instinct to attack and dissect the unknown—useful for debugging systems but damaging in interpersonal contexts. By getting curious instead of defensive, leaders can honor their need for clarity while creating psychological safety. This approach enables learning organizations where people feel safe to fail and new ideas can flourish.
key_points: Aggressive skepticism that serves engineers well in debugging systems can come across as 'obnoxious trolling' in interpersonal interactions
'Get curious' means assuming others have context you're missing rather than assuming they haven't considered your objections
Curiosity creates psychological safety—it enables teams to discuss failures without blame
This approach is a 'bigger picture version' of the improv technique 'Yes, and...' that the author finds easier to maintain
Curiosity is effective even when you need to challenge others—it creates change more effectively than aggression
money_quotes: Whether by virtue of personality or experience, my instincts always lead me to attack and take apart the unknown.
Unfortunately, when it comes to interpersonal interactions, aggressively looking for flaws in things you don't understand looks less like smart defensive behavior and more like obnoxious trolling.
Instead of assuming that the other party hasn't thought of your objections, try to understand deeply the context in which they are making their statements.
The possibility that you're missing something important. And that openness makes people want to work with you.
Curiosity in the face of failure, instead of blame, leads to a safe environment for people to fail, and lets us discuss our failures.
You'll be surprised at how much more effectively you can create change when you approach situations with open curiosity.
key_insight: Replacing aggressive skepticism with genuine curiosity allows leaders to maintain intellectual rigor while creating the psychological safety teams need to take risks and learn from failure.
